title: Wikimedia Brief
subtitle: What Wikimedia needs to understand about Unicode and SignWriting
summary: A short practical note for Wikimedia staff and collaborators about why naming characters is not enough for current SignWriting project needs.
synopsis: This brief is for Wikimedia and other public-platform readers. It explains why current SignWriting work cannot rely on official Unicode SignWriting alone, why the real gap is larger than font support, how the facial system affects stable symbol identity, what the current Sutton SignWriting production stack uses instead, and what a realistic public posture looks like without flattening the problem into a solved-symbol-block story.

slides.title: What Wikimedia Needs To Know First
slides.summary: The Unicode issue is still practical for Wikimedia projects that want to support SignWriting well.
bullets: Official Unicode SignWriting is not sufficient by itself
Current projects still depend on FSW and SWU
Naming characters is not enough for current project needs
The issue is encoding plus interoperability, not only fonts
That matters for storage, reuse, and maintenance
slides.speech: Wikimedia's starting point should be simple. The Unicode issue around SignWriting is still practical and active. Official Unicode SignWriting is not sufficient by itself for current project needs. Current work still depends on FSW and SWU. Naming characters is not enough for the real storage, reuse, and maintenance needs of Wikimedia projects. The issue is not only fonts. It is also encoding, compatibility, and interoperability across real project workflows.
slides.notes: Open collaboratively. This is the shortest honest framing.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Wikimedia project need] --> B[Storage]
  A --> C[Rendering]
  A --> D[Reuse and sharing]

slides.title: What Wikimedia Is Likely To Notice
slides.summary: Wikimedia projects usually encounter the problem first as custom strings, scripts, and uncertainty about whether Unicode already solved it.
bullets: ASCII-based strings in real project work
Custom renderers or user scripts
Mixed assumptions about Unicode support
These are symptoms, not the whole architecture
slides.speech: What Wikimedia is likely to notice first is not the whole architecture. It is the symptom layer. People see ASCII-based strings, custom rendering, and mixed assumptions about whether Unicode already solved the problem. That confusion is understandable. The public expectation formed around the block. The project reality still depends on other layers. These are symptoms of a deeper compatibility gap, not the whole diagnosis by themselves.
slides.notes: Make this recognizable to a Wikimedia audience.
slides.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A[Project reality] --> B[ASCII strings]
  A --> C[Custom rendering]
  A --> D[Unicode confusion]
  B --> E[Need for clearer technical baseline]
  C --> E
  D --> E

  classDef node fill:#eff6ff,color:black,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px;
  class A,B,C,D,E node;

slides.title: What The Real Gap Is
slides.summary: The real gap is not only missing fonts. It includes stable symbol identity, written-sign encoding, and interoperability.
bullets: The issue is larger than rendering
Naming characters is not the same as preserving stable symbols
The official model does not provide a compatible written-sign solution
The facial system shifts part of symbol formation into font behavior
Data storage, comparison, and reuse are involved
slides.speech: The real gap is not only that some fonts are missing. The larger problem is that naming characters is not the same as preserving stable symbols, and the current official model also does not provide a compatible written-sign solution. In the facial system, part of symbol formation shifts into font behavior. That affects not only rendering, but also how data is stored, compared, and reused across systems. This is why the problem matters to Wikimedia as infrastructure rather than only as display.
slides.notes: This is the core explanatory slide.

slides.title: What The Current Stack Uses
slides.summary: Current Sutton SignWriting workflows rely on FSW and SWU because that stack already carries the real ecosystem.
bullets: FSW is the main production encoding
SWU is the supported Unicode-oriented isomorphic form
Current tools and datasets are built around them
Treat that production reality as the baseline
slides.speech: If Wikimedia wants to describe or handle SignWriting accurately, it helps to start from the real production stack rather than from the assumption that the official block already solved the problem. Current Sutton SignWriting workflows rely on FSW and SWU. That is not an awkward exception. It is the present baseline for tools, datasets, and interoperability.
slides.notes: Use this to keep the conversation grounded.

slides.title: What A Realistic Public Posture Looks Like
slides.summary: Public discussion is most useful when it starts from the actual production stack and avoids claiming the problem is already solved.
bullets: Establish a shared technical baseline
Avoid flattening the issue into a solved-symbol-block story
Recognize FSW and SWU as the practical baseline
Revisit Unicode only from an accurate technical foundation
slides.speech: A realistic public posture begins by acknowledging the current production stack rather than assuming the Unicode block already solved the problem. FSW and SWU remain the practical baseline. Accurate understanding matters more than premature standards conclusions, and any future Unicode discussion is only worth the effort if it begins from that reality.
slides.notes: End with clarity and restraint, not with an ask for support.
